stem: A 4-week-old male infant is brought to the clinic by his mother, who is concerned about his persistent yellowish skin discolouration. He was born at 39 weeks gestation. His newborn screening test was reported as normal, and he received his birth dose of hepatitis B and vitamin K intramuscularly. His mother had an uncomplicated pregnancy but required a brief course of oral antibiotics for a presumed urinary tract infection at 36 weeks. The infant is exclusively breastfed, feeding every 3 hours, and producing 6 - 8 wet nappies per day. His stools have become noticeably pale and chalky over the past week, and his urine leaves a dark yellow stain on the nappy. On examination, the infant is alert and vigorous, with a weight of 4.2 kg (birth weight 3.4 kg). He has frank scleral icterus and widespread cutaneous jaundice extending to the umbilicus. Abdominal examination reveals a smooth, firm liver edge palpable 3 cm below the right costal margin. A formal venous blood panel is obtained.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure the mother and continue exclusive breastfeeding.
options.rationale: While breast milk jaundice is a common cause of prolonged neonatal jaundice, it presents with unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia; this infant's conjugated fraction is severely elevated. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)

options.text: Commence intensive blue-light phototherapy immediately.
options.rationale: Phototherapy is only indicated for unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia. In cholestasis, it is ineffective and can cause 'bronze baby syndrome'. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)

options.text: Prescribe a trial of oral ursodeoxycholic acid.
options.rationale: Although used for cholestasis, empirical treatment without ruling out a time-critical surgical emergency (biliary atresia) is inappropriate and delays definitive care. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)

options.text: Arrange an outpatient hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid scan.
options.rationale: A HIDA scan is part of the workup, but an abdominal ultrasound must be performed first to assess structural anatomy, and the timeline must be urgent, not routine outpatient. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)

options.text: Admit for an urgent fasting abdominal ultrasound.
options.rationale: Pale stools, dark urine, and a conjugated bilirubin fraction >20% of total indicate pathological cholestasis. Biliary atresia must be urgently excluded via ultrasound as first-line imaging. (Source: RCH CPG: Jaundice in early infancy)

stem: A 3-year-old girl is rushed to the emergency department by ambulance. Her parents report a 12-hour history of high fever, extreme lethargy, and vomiting. She is fully immunised to schedule. On arrival, she is poorly responsive, groaning to pain. She is notably pale with mottled skin and a non-blanching purpuric rash spreading rapidly across her lower limbs and abdomen. Her extremities are cold to the touch with a capillary refill time of 5 seconds. Two wide-bore intravenous cannulae are immediately secured. Initial point-of-care testing shows a lactate of 5.0 mmol/L. She is immediately given a 20 mL/kg rapid bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride and intravenous ceftriaxone. Reassessment 15 minutes later shows no clinical improvement, and a second 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride is administered (total 40 mL/kg given). On repeat assessment, she remains obtunded, capillary refill is 4 seconds, and her blood pressure continues to fall.
lead_in: What is the single best next intervention?

options.text: Administer a third 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride.
options.rationale: In paediatric sepsis, fluid-refractory shock is defined as persisting shock after 40 mL/kg of crystalloid. Further boluses increase the risk of pulmonary oedema without correcting the vasoplegia. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Perform immediate rapid sequence endotracheal intubation.
options.rationale: While she is obtunded, intubating a profoundly shocked child before restoring vascular tone with inotropes is extremely high risk for peri-intubation cardiac arrest due to loss of sympathetic drive. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Commence a peripheral intravenous adrenaline infusion.
options.rationale: The child has fluid-refractory 'cold' septic shock (likely meningococcal). Australian guidelines mandate the early commencement of inotropes (adrenaline) peripherally while awaiting central access. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Administer a 10 mL/kg bolus of 4% human albumin solution.
options.rationale: Colloids are not superior to crystalloids in initial sepsis resuscitation. Furthermore, her shock is fluid-refractory, meaning vasoactive agents, not more volume, are the physiological priority. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

options.text: Administer a stat dose of intravenous hydrocortisone sodium.
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are indicated for suspected adrenal insufficiency or catecholamine-resistant shock, but only AFTER an adrenaline/noradrenaline infusion has been commenced and optimised. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis)

stem: A 6-week-old term infant is brought to the emergency department in mid-winter with 3 days of coryza, cough, and increasing work of breathing. He was born at 39 weeks, has been feeding half his usual volume for 24 hours, and has had 2 wet nappies today. He is fully immunised for age, has no congenital heart disease, and takes no regular medicines. His older sibling has a runny nose, and he attends daycare. On examination he is alert but tired, with subcostal recessions, fine widespread crackles, and wheeze. He has no focal chest signs. He was born with a mild tongue tie that has not affected feeding. His mother asks whether he needs antibiotics because a cousin with pneumonia was admitted last month. This infant remains on room air but is becoming more tachypnoeic.
lead_in: **What is the single best next step in management?**

options.text: Start oral amoxicillin and arrange outpatient review tomorrow
options.rationale: This is not a focal bacterial pneumonia picture, and routine antibiotics do not improve typical bronchiolitis. The key management issue here is supportive care with close observation, not community-acquired pneumonia treatment. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Provide suction, oxygen if needed, and support oral or nasogastric fluids
options.rationale: This infant has typical bronchiolitis with mild hypoxia, feeding reduction, and increased work of breathing, so supportive care is the appropriate next step. Oxygen and hydration are the mainstays, while antibiotics are not indicated in uncomplicated bronchiolitis. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Order a chest x-ray and commence intravenous ceftriaxone
options.rationale: Typical bronchiolitis does not need routine imaging or empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics. The absence of focal chest signs and the age-appropriate viral prodrome point away from bacterial consolidation. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Give nebulised salbutamol and reassess after 20 minutes
options.rationale: Bronchodilators are not recommended for routine bronchiolitis management because they do not meaningfully change outcomes in typical cases. Wheeze alone does not make this asthma. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis.

options.text: Administer dexamethasone and nebulised adrenaline
options.rationale: This is a lower-airway bronchiolitis presentation rather than croup, as there is no barking cough or stridor. Steroids and nebulised adrenaline are used for croup, not routine bronchiolitis. Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis; RCH CPG: Croup.

stem: A 10-year-old boy with type 1 diabetes diagnosed 18 months ago is brought to the emergency department after 24 hours of vomiting and lethargy. His insulin pump tubing disconnected overnight and he has had progressive abdominal pain and thirst since morning. He received a correction bolus by pen 2 hours ago. On arrival he is drowsy but obeys commands. During initial treatment with isotonic fluids and intravenous insulin, he becomes increasingly irritable and complains of severe headache. His mother reports he is now asking repetitive questions. Capillary glucose has fallen from 28.6 mmol/L to 18.2 mmol/L over 90 minutes.
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate immediate next step?**

options.text: Reduce insulin infusion rate and repeat venous blood gas in 30 minutes before further action
options.rationale: Biochemical reassessment is important, but new neurologic deterioration during DKA should prompt immediate treatment for cerebral oedema rather than observation. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Administer hyperosmolar therapy and urgently escalate for presumed cerebral oedema
options.rationale: Headache, irritability, altered mental status, and repetitive questioning during DKA treatment strongly suggest cerebral oedema. Immediate hyperosmolar treatment and senior escalation are indicated. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Increase isotonic fluid replacement because persistent dehydration is the likely explanation
options.rationale: Excessive fluid administration may worsen cerebral oedema risk. Neurologic decline in this context should not be treated as simple dehydration progression. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)

options.text: Arrange urgent CT brain before initiating further treatment to confirm diagnosis
options.rationale: Imaging should not delay treatment when cerebral oedema is clinically suspected during DKA management. Treatment is time critical. (Source: RCH CPG Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

options.text: Cease insulin and commence dextrose-containing fluids as the glucose is falling rapidly
options.rationale: Dextrose may be added later to allow continued insulin, but stopping insulin risks worsening ketosis and does not address acute neurologic deterioration. (Source: ISPAD DKA Guideline)

stem: A 10-month-old boy is brought to the ED with 8 hours of intermittent screaming episodes, drawing up of the legs, and repeated non-bilious vomiting. Between episodes he is pale and settles briefly, but he has become increasingly lethargic. His mother says he had a mild viral illness last week. He was born at term, has no previous surgery, and his immunisations are up to date. On examination he is afebrile, mildly dehydrated, and has a soft but mildly distended abdomen. A small amount of red mucus is seen in the nappy after a pain episode. His father asks whether this could be gastroenteritis because several children at daycare were unwell. There is no bilious vomiting and no peritonism.
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate initial investigation?**

options.text: Abdominal ultrasound
options.rationale: This presentation is classic for intussusception, and ultrasound is the preferred initial investigation. The episodic pain, lethargy, vomiting, and red mucus all support that pathway. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.

options.text: Plain abdominal x-ray
options.rationale: A plain x-ray can show obstruction or perforation, but it is not the best first test when intussusception is suspected and the child is stable. Ultrasound is more accurate for diagnosis. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.

options.text: Urgent upper gastrointestinal contrast study
options.rationale: An upper GI study is used for malrotation or volvulus, especially with bilious vomiting. This child has intermittent colicky pain and red mucus instead, which points to intussusception. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.

options.text: CT abdomen with intravenous contrast
options.rationale: CT is not the usual initial investigation in children with suspected intussusception because it adds radiation and is not needed when ultrasound is available. Bedside ultrasound is the preferred test. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.

options.text: Stool PCR panel for infectious gastroenteritis
options.rationale: The colicky episodic pain and red mucus are not explained well by routine gastroenteritis. A stool test would delay diagnosis and treatment of a surgical emergency. Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception.

stem: A 7-year-old girl receiving chemotherapy for acute lymphoblastic leukaemia presents with fever and malaise. She had her last vincristine and dexamethasone cycle 6 days ago and now has worsening tiredness, reduced oral intake, and mild abdominal discomfort. Her central venous line is clean at the skin but has not been accessed in 2 days. She takes trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis and ondansetron as needed. Her parents report no cough, sore throat, or urinary symptoms, but she had one episode of loose stool this morning. On examination she looks unwell but is not in respiratory distress. She is pale, has cool peripheries, and appears tachycardic. Her oncology team advises that she may be neutropenic. There is no focal source of infection, and she has no drug allergies.
lead_in: **What is the single best next step in management?**

options.text: Take blood cultures and start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics immediately
options.rationale: This child has febrile neutropenia with systemic features, so urgent empiric intravenous antibiotics after appropriate cultures is the correct next step. Treatment should not be delayed while searching for a source. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.

options.text: Observe for 4 hours and repeat FBC before treating
options.rationale: This is unsafe because fever with severe neutropenia is a medical emergency. Waiting for repeat blood counts delays time-critical antibiotics. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.

options.text: Start oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and discharge if stable
options.rationale: Oral therapy and discharge are not appropriate for a febrile child with profound neutropenia and raised inflammatory markers. She needs urgent intravenous treatment and admission. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.

options.text: Remove the central venous line before cultures are taken
options.rationale: Line removal is not the first step in an otherwise line-preserved febrile neutropenia presentation. Cultures should be obtained promptly, and antibiotics started immediately. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.

options.text: Give granulocyte colony-stimulating factor and await marrow recovery
options.rationale: G-CSF does not replace urgent empirical antibiotics in febrile neutropenia. The immediate priority is antimicrobial therapy and sepsis management. Source: RCH Fever and suspected or confirmed neutropenia.

stem: A 13-year-old girl with type 1 diabetes is brought in with 18 hours of abdominal pain, vomiting, and increasing lethargy after a viral illness. She has missed two doses of insulin because she was not eating well and thought it would be safer to stop it. Her mother says she has been drinking large volumes of water and passing urine frequently for 2 days. She is drowsy but rousable, has deep breathing, and smells strongly of ketones. She has no history of asthma or renal disease. She takes basal-bolus insulin and uses a continuous glucose monitor, which has been reading high. There is no focal abdominal tenderness, and pregnancy testing is negative. Her parents ask whether she can have insulin first because her sugar is very high. The team needs the safest immediate plan.
lead_in: **What is the single best next step in management?**

options.text: Start insulin infusion immediately without fluids
options.rationale: Insulin is essential in DKA, but fluids and careful resuscitation come first or alongside initial management. Starting insulin without addressing intravascular depletion is unsafe. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: Give a normal saline bolus and begin the paediatric DKA protocol
options.rationale: This child has moderate to severe DKA with dehydration, acidosis, and ketosis, so the correct next step is fluid resuscitation and a standard DKA protocol. Insulin is then started in a controlled manner after initial fluids and assessment. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: Administer subcutaneous rapid-acting insulin and discharge if ketones fall
options.rationale: Subcutaneous insulin is not appropriate for this degree of acidosis, dehydration, and altered mental state. She requires hospital-based DKA management. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: Give sodium bicarbonate because the pH is below 7.20
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is not routinely used in paediatric DKA and is reserved for very specific circumstances. Standard therapy is fluids, insulin, and close biochemical monitoring. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

options.text: Treat hyperkalaemia with calcium gluconate before any fluids
options.rationale: Her potassium is elevated but typical DKA management focuses on controlled rehydration and insulin, with electrolyte monitoring and replacement as needed. There is no indication here for emergency calcium first. Source: RCH Diabetic ketoacidosis.

stem: An 8-month-old ex-35-week infant is brought to the emergency department in winter with 4 days of cough, coryza, and poor feeding. He was reviewed by a GP yesterday and diagnosed with bronchiolitis. Overnight he took less than half his usual feeds and had two episodes of post-tussive vomiting. His mother reports increasing work of breathing and fewer wet nappies today. He has no history of congenital heart disease but required CPAP briefly after birth. In the emergency department he is started on low-flow oxygen via nasal prongs with transient improvement in saturations. Thirty minutes later he remains tachypnoeic with persistent subcostal and intercostal recession. Capillary blood gas shows mild hypercapnia without acidosis. A junior doctor suggests trialling salbutamol because there is occasional wheeze on auscultation and the father has asthma.
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**

options.text: Commence high-flow nasal prong therapy and continue supportive management with nasogastric hydration
options.rationale: Persistent hypoxia and moderate work of breathing despite low-flow oxygen indicate need for escalation to high-flow nasal prong therapy. Supportive care remains the cornerstone of bronchiolitis management. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Trial inhaled salbutamol and reassess respiratory rate before considering respiratory support escalation
options.rationale: Routine bronchodilator trials are not recommended in typical bronchiolitis because they do not reliably improve clinically important outcomes. Escalation of respiratory support is more appropriate. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone because the hypercapnia suggests developing bacterial pneumonia
options.rationale: Mild hypercapnia may occur with worsening bronchiolitis and does not itself suggest bacterial pneumonia. There are no focal findings or septic features supporting empiric antibiotics. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory)

options.text: Arrange urgent intubation because preterm birth increases risk of sudden respiratory decompensation
options.rationale: Although prematurity increases bronchiolitis severity risk, this infant does not yet show impending respiratory arrest, severe acidosis, or exhaustion requiring immediate intubation. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Discharge home with frequent oral fluids because oxygen saturations improved on nasal prongs
options.rationale: Persistent tachypnoea, increased work of breathing, poor oral intake, and oxygen requirement make discharge unsafe. Ongoing inpatient supportive care is indicated. (Source: RCH CPG Bronchiolitis)

stem: A 5-year-old boy is brought to a regional emergency department with fever, vomiting, and progressive lethargy over 12 hours. Three children at his childcare centre were recently absent with febrile illnesses. He returned from visiting extended family in rural Queensland 5 days ago. His parents report he complained of leg pain earlier today and became difficult to wake after an afternoon nap. He has no significant medical history and is fully immunised according to the National Immunisation Program. On arrival he is confused and intermittently agitated. A blanching maculopapular rash on his trunk has evolved over the past hour into scattered non-blanching purpuric lesions over the lower limbs. Peripheral IV access is difficult and blood cultures have not yet been obtained. During assessment he develops cool peripheries and prolonged capillary refill. A junior doctor asks whether ceftriaxone should be delayed until cultures are secured because lumbar puncture may also be needed urgently.
lead_in: **What is the single best next step in management?**

options.text: Perform urgent lumbar puncture before antibiotics because meningitis confirmation will guide therapy duration
options.rationale: Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability and should not delay treatment of suspected meningococcal sepsis. Immediate empiric therapy is required. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)

options.text: Administer empiric intravenous ceftriaxone immediately while initiating aggressive septic shock resuscitation
options.rationale: This child has meningococcal septic shock with purpura, hypotension, and elevated lactate. Empiric antibiotics and sepsis resuscitation must not be delayed for cultures or lumbar puncture. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)

options.text: Obtain blood cultures and CT brain before antimicrobial therapy because altered consciousness raises concern for raised ICP
options.rationale: Neuroimaging and cultures are secondary priorities in unstable septic shock. Delaying antibiotics increases mortality in invasive meningococcal disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence aciclovir and transfer for MRI because encephalitis is more likely than bacterial sepsis
options.rationale: Purpura, shock physiology, prolonged capillary refill, and elevated lactate strongly support meningococcal sepsis rather than isolated viral encephalitis. (Source: RCH CPG Meningococcal Infection)

options.text: Administer intramuscular benzylpenicillin and observe response before escalating to intravenous therapy
options.rationale: Pre-hospital IM benzylpenicillin may be considered in remote settings, but this critically unwell child requires immediate definitive intravenous therapy and full septic shock management. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

stem: A 13-year-old boy with moderate persistent asthma presents to the emergency department with worsening shortness of breath over 8 hours after developing rhinorrhoea and cough 2 days earlier. He used salbutamol every 2 hours overnight with progressively shorter relief. He was admitted to hospital twice last year and required PICU admission at age 9 but has never been intubated. His preventer adherence is poor. On arrival he can speak only in short phrases and prefers to sit upright. He receives inhaled salbutamol and ipratropium with oral dexamethasone. Ninety minutes later he remains tachypnoeic with increasing fatigue. Air entry is now reduced bilaterally and wheeze is quieter than at presentation. A venous blood gas is repeated because nursing staff are concerned he appears more drowsy.
lead_in: **What is the most appropriate next step in management?**

options.text: Continue intermittent salbutamol only because the oxygen saturation has improved with supplemental oxygen
options.rationale: Improved oxygen saturation alone does not indicate clinical stability. Rising carbon dioxide and fatigue suggest impending respiratory failure requiring escalation. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)

options.text: Arrange discharge with prednisolone because reduced wheeze indicates treatment response
options.rationale: A quieter chest with worsening hypercapnia and fatigue is concerning for severe airflow limitation rather than improvement. Discharge would be unsafe. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory)

options.text: Escalate to intensive care review for intravenous therapy and possible respiratory support
options.rationale: Rising pCO2, fatigue, altered mental state, and reduced air entry indicate life-threatening asthma with impending respiratory failure requiring PICU-level escalation. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)

options.text: Administer intravenous antibiotics because viral-triggered bacterial pneumonia is now most likely
options.rationale: There are no focal infective findings supporting bacterial pneumonia. The major issue is worsening ventilatory failure from severe asthma. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Respiratory)

options.text: Cease bronchodilators and commence non-invasive ventilation on the ward because tachycardia suggests toxicity
options.rationale: Tachycardia is common after beta-agonists and severe respiratory distress. Bronchodilators should not be ceased in deteriorating acute severe asthma. (Source: RCH CPG Acute Asthma)
